text: Yep, yep, the classic "world of expression vs world of statements" distinction. I too think about ideas like this a lot. My current perspective on this is greatly informed by Conal Elliot, and this post in particular (which I had to read half a dozen times to grok): <http://conal.net/blog/posts/can-functional-programming-be-liberated-from-the-von-neumann-paradigm>

Autodesk have a research division that produces some works useful in my own visual programming & simulation research. Nothing special, though — similar to what you'd get out of all the research universities and other companies building _tools as products_. <https://autodeskresearch.com>

Have you thought more about what exactly that graph structure could look like?
Have you thought more about what exactly that graph structure could look like?
Yeah, I just omitted it to not overcomplicate my post. Wait a sec.
Yeah, I just omitted it to not overcomplicate my post. Wait a sec.

I thought how versioning could look like and realized that it takes two kind of edges:

1) hard edges. Similar to relationships between parent and child commits in git. When hard edge created, destination node takes id of source node as part of input to hashing function for id. Hard edges can be created only simultaneously with destination node.

2) soft edges. These edges can be created at any time and don't participate in hash generation for nodes.

Basically graph is bunch of DAGs that we know from git, yet nodes may have more edges between each other providing extra information.

1) hard edges. Similar to relationships between parent and child commits in git. When hard edge created, destination node takes id of source node as part of input to hashing function for id. Hard edges can be created only simultaneously with destination node.

2) soft edges. These edges can be created at any time and don't participate in hash generation for nodes.

Basically graph is bunch of DAGs that we know from git, yet nodes may have more edges between each other providing extra information.

I thought about node content and how viewers should understand how to interpret it. I realized that single node might be interpreted differently in different contexts.

Say if node represents some historical fact, then in timeline viewer it would be represented by timestamp, on world map it will be represented by shapes on the map (where it happened).

Or say we got function that might be interpreted as text code, or as some element of visual program that requires some additional info not available in text code. It should be part of one node.

So different kinds of data, probably for different applications should be stored in one node.
I came up with idea of having namespaces, where application author's public key serves as namespace for node attribute.

I came up with idea of having namespaces, where application author's public key serves as namespace for node attribute.

Thanks for reading my blog, <@UCKRZS3DZ> :smile:. I'm still trying to think these things through. My belief is that we need to think about this from a holistic, whole-system perspective and rethink whether the prevalent concepts are even necessary.

I like the ideas you are proposing and I'll add some of my thoughts below. This is absolutely about a new and different kind of hypermedia, and *not* a replication of physical paper in a digital machine (which is what text files are). I do think a *single* location-independent, append-only graph like system could be used to hold *all* static structural things - code (if we need it), rich media, and all their interconnections, sub parts and versions.

All replication can be orthogonal - whether its for caching a part of this graph to a user's local machine or for redundancy. We can have just a single namespace that can precisely point to any object in this world. Alan Kay once said something like 'give every object a URL'.

Then we can think about built-in concepts, i.e metadata that this system supports. Something like 'new version' is just a special kind of edge. So is 'derived from' (e.g. I use a tool to convert a picture to grayscale, the new picture should be linked to the old, and to the function/tool that modified it). Even 'type-of' could be an edge that links a media object to a transcoder object - you shouldn't have to go hunting around for 'programs' that will decode a 'file' you have. Any dynamic/executable objects should link to *all* objects that form the interpreters/runtimes/dependencies that it needs - no more runarounds trying to recreate the environment from reading READMEs or whatever. IOW, preserve the connection to the *context*.

Optimizations and compromises (such as discarding edges) should be defined separately, rather than designed in (we want a system that is limited by computer constraints only, not by design).

I also think it is important not to build in assumptions like programming paradigms etc, so many kinds can be supported. Of course, much of the work is in the details.

BTW, some related ideas are in the <http://unisonweb.org|unisonweb.org> and in <http://infra-structure.org|infra-structure.org>.

Thanks for reading my blog, <@UCKRZS3DZ> :smile:. I'm still trying to think these things through. My belief is that we need to think about this from a holistic, whole-system perspective and rethink whether the prevalent concepts are even necessary.

I like the ideas you are proposing and I'll add some of my thoughts below. This is absolutely about a new and different kind of hypermedia, and *not* a replication of physical paper in a digital machine (which is what text files are). I do think a *single* location-independent, append-only graph like system could be used to hold *all* static structural things - code (if we need it), rich media, and all their interconnections, sub parts and versions.

All replication can be orthogonal - whether its for caching a part of this graph to a user's local machine or for redundancy. We can have just a single namespace that can precisely point to any object in this world. Alan Kay once said something like 'give every object a URL'.

Then we can think about built-in concepts, i.e metadata that this system supports. Something like 'new version' is just a special kind of edge. So is 'derived from' (e.g. I use a tool to convert a picture to grayscale, the new picture should be linked to the old, and to the function/tool that modified it). Even 'type-of' could be an edge that links a media object to a transcoder object - you shouldn't have to go hunting around for 'programs' that will decode a 'file' you have. Any dynamic/executable objects should link to *all* objects that form the interpreters/runtimes/dependencies that it needs - no more runarounds trying to recreate the environment from reading READMEs or whatever. IOW, preserve the connection to the *context*.

Optimizations and compromises (such as discarding edges) should be defined separately, rather than designed in (we want a system that is limited by computer constraints only, not by design).

I also think it is important not to build in assumptions like programming paradigms etc, so many kinds can be supported. Of course, much of the work is in the details.

BTW, some related ideas are in the <http://unisonweb.org|unisonweb.org> and in <http://infra-structure.org|infra-structure.org>.

And by 'rethink the prevalent concepts' I'm talking about things like 'programs' - a fundamental concept but why do we need it? In the end we want systems with desired behaviors. But a system is not a  program! Most system behavior emerges from the composition of multiple programs, where the parts of a program are composed together very differently than how multiple programs are composed together (and how multiple clusters of programs are composed together). Another perspective could be we inspect and modify the system through various on-demand projections.

attachments.fallback:
[September 3rd, 2018 5:46 PM] niko.elmari: <@U8A5MS6R1> If we think the requirement of tokens from the abstract data structure point of view:

-As you stated: text files are a list of strings. Therefore we can have many things on the single level.
-To be able to compose things from smaller parts we need a tree. This level lives usually on the syntax level.
-Next, we need to be able to reuse existing parts elsewhere so we need DAG. Currently done with tokens on higher level inside the compiler.
-Cyclic graphs for recursive definitions? - out of scope for now

One point here is that while you can represent the general program as graph we still represent some of the program structure as a nested tree and rest with tokens. Even tough program can be considered to be composed from equal class relations. So nesting is quite cheap and obvious way of improvement but yeah it kind is just halfway to the natural structure of programming languages.

So do we need tokens as an addition for nesting? I think we need the structure which has native support for at least DAGs. The most direct way of thinking next incremental step is adding tokens in addition to nesting. However, then there are two different ways of referencing. That smells to my nose a bit.

My own current favorite structure model is to use GUID/hash identified tuples (or lists) which have in cells either payload data or reference. Therefore there is the only single way of having substructure which also supports graphs. Visualizing / editing can then happen by nesting and by token (label by metadata of token) and this visualization doesn't have to be static even tough actual structure is untouched.

Btw your blog post: Files, Formats and Byte Arrays was great area capture and caused that "of course"-realization. Files require same kind metastructure where referencing to other files is standardized. But if files contain graph which can have references to another same kind of files (which lives in a hierarchical filesystem), what is the value of file concept? It starts to smell like low-level implementation detail which I maybe don't want to see at all. Some time ago I relistened Future Of Coding E10 - Interview of Paul Chiusano and got idea/illusion that it should be possible to make application persistency completely invisible by using the right abstraction. Elm family-architectures with time-traveling debugger are some sort of proof.

text: When we're talking about immutability and content-addressed data, I feel like I should mention <https://ipfs.io/>. Wouldn't it be cool if one day public data / code / programs would be shared in a distibuted peer-to-peer network like ipfs?

attachments.title: IPFS is the Distributed Web
attachments.text: A peer-to-peer hypermedia protocol to make the web faster, safer, and more open.

text: Not sure where to draw the line here between proper research communities and more pragmatic forums where people like us meet online. Also not sure how much I care about that distinction. I'm kind of just trying to keep an overview of what's out there. Obviously, a part of me has this dream that we all at one point find the perfect medium to discuss these things and come to gather in a single place, but… you know… yeah…

Not sure where to draw the line here between proper research communities and more pragmatic forums where people like us meet online. Also not sure how much I care about that distinction. I'm kind of just trying to keep an overview of what's out there. Obviously, a part of me has this dream that we all at one point find the perfect medium to discuss these things and come to gather in a single place, but… you know… yeah…
It seems like everyone in these communities is also on Twitter. Not sure I’d call it perfect, but it is effective.
It seems like everyone in these communities is also on Twitter. Not sure I’d call it perfect, but it is effective.

text: Just realized that Realtalk in Dynamicland is exactly that pair of languages that I described. It has Managing Language with its Claim, When and Wish IO instructions. It has Lua as a Calculating Language, which code can be treated as a pure function by Managing Language.

attachments.fallback:
<https://twitter.com/vladimir_vg|@vladimir_vg>: A bit about programming languages.

It feels like there are two distinct programming languages that PL designers habitually mix into one.

1) Calculating language. It's code lives inside box with explicitly specified inputs and outputs. There is no IO, nor time inside the box.

text: Keep in mind they aren't wedded to the particular tech.  Lua is clean and small.  As for claiming and wishing, the goal is to make it so that there's very loose coupling between modules — or really sheets of paper.  One reason you don't see a lot written about Realtalk is that they don't want people mistaking it for Dynamicland.

Keep in mind they aren't wedded to the particular tech.  Lua is clean and small.  As for claiming and wishing, the goal is to make it so that there's very loose coupling between modules — or really sheets of paper.  One reason you don't see a lot written about Realtalk is that they don't want people mistaking it for Dynamicland.

Humane Tech Community:

<https://community.humanetech.com>

What I like about this is that it's mostly driven by stakeholders OTHER than programmers, who have recognize the need to improve the state of software / programming. That's invaluable perspective, since us tech guys are sometimes biased by our own needs vs how software can be changed to better serve humanity (business, users). I'm very hopeful that bringing more of "us" into their org may have great potential to bridge that gap and do the most good

attachments.title: Humane Tech Community
attachments.text: A place to discuss all things regarding the intersection between tech and humanity.

text:
It would be GREAT if we could make one wiki or tree or catalog of all these organizations & projects, perhaps with tags so they can be related by topic / feature / concept.

However, some of theses resources already *are* such an attempt ... Is it possible that we/they could try to come together to make one much more well-done such thing?

I'd certainly be willing to dissolve "SomethingNew" into a larger such effort if it meant a more unified effort

Do we really _want_ a hub-and-spokes model? That sort of centralization is very "cathedral". Me, I prefer to embrace the distributed essence of the web, and say that a webring/graph of interlinking sites is fine. Sure, you won't have all of these projects under one beautifully painted dome roof, but you also won't run into the problem of needing to decide what belongs and what doesn't (eg: I like to link to video games). And besides — nobody has time to actually read _all_ this stuff _and_ make programming tools of the future, right? (Not just me?)
